title: Cat Weight Loss Despite Increased Appetite: Causes and Veterinary Advice
meta_desc: Is your cat eating more but losing weight? Learn about common causes like hyperthyroidism and diabetes, and why this requires a veterinary visit.
content_html:
TL;DR: If your cat is losing weight despite a ravenous appetite, it likely indicates a metabolic or endocrine disorder that requires a veterinary exam within the next 48 to 72 hours.

What does it mean if my cat is losing weight but has an increased appetite?

When a cat displays a ravenous appetite, a condition known as polyphagia, yet continues to lose body mass, it suggests that their body is either failing to absorb nutrients or is burning calories at an abnormally high rate. This 'starving while eating' scenario is a classic clinical sign of several metabolic or endocrine disorders that require medical intervention.

Is weight loss with an increased appetite considered a veterinary emergency?

  • The urgency level for weight loss with an increased appetite is classified as Medium.
  • It is generally not an immediate, life-threatening emergency unless accompanied by acute collapse or respiratory distress.
  • Because it signals a significant underlying health issue, you should schedule a veterinary appointment within the next 48 to 72 hours to prevent further deterioration of muscle mass and organ function.

How can a photo or video help the veterinarian triage my cat’s condition?

  • A clear 'bird's-eye view' photo taken from above while the cat is standing helps the vet assess the Body Condition Score (BCS) and identify muscle wasting along the spine or hips.
  • Photos of the litter box can provide clues about secondary symptoms like diarrhea.
  • A video of your cat's eating behavior or water intake can help identify issues like excessive thirst that help narrow down the potential cause.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats, weight loss despite increased appetite (polyphagia) can be a key indicator of several underlying conditions. Hyperthyroidism is a common cause, often accompanied by hyperexcitability, polydipsia, polyuria, and potentially gastrointestinal signs such as vomiting and diarrhea, as well as cardiovascular signs. Malabsorption syndromes should also be considered, even in the absence of diarrhea, and can be associated with coprophagia and pica. A thorough diagnostic approach is warranted to differentiate malabsorption from other systemic and metabolic diseases.

Chapter: Endocrinology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 559)

faqs.question: What are the most common causes for this condition?
faqs.answer: The most frequent causes in cats are hyperthyroidism (an overactive thyroid), diabetes mellitus, inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), and intestinal parasites.

faqs.question: What diagnostic tests will my veterinarian perform?
faqs.answer: Expect your vet to recommend a full blood panel, a thyroid level check (T4), and a urinalysis to evaluate organ function and glucose levels.

faqs.question: Is weight loss in older cats just a normal part of aging?
faqs.answer: No, significant weight loss is never a normal part of aging. While senior cats may lose some muscle tone, sudden weight loss despite a good appetite always warrants a medical exam.
